title: $select dollar-sign escape in double-quoted strings
message: PowerShell expands $select in double quotes. Use backtick to escape.

title: Unprefixed assignment shadows script
message: Unprefixed assignment at script scope shadows $script:. Use a different name.

title: --force-overwrite does not resolve metadata conflicts
message: Use --force-overwrite only for property-level reconciliation. Delete conflicting attributes before import.

title: Connect-CrmOnlineDiscovery requires WinPS 5.1
message: These modules hang in pwsh. Ensure script has #Requires -Version 5.1 or #Requires -PSEdition Desktop and runs under powershell.exe.

title: Boolean column body missing TrueOption/FalseOption
message: BooleanAttributeMetadata requires both TrueOption and FalseOption in the body.

title: pac solution import missing --publish-changes
message: pac solution import should include --publish-changes to ensure customizations are active. A successful import without this flag leaves customizations unpublished and the deployment is half-broken.

title: Topological Stage Ordering
message: Managed solution import should precede table/column creation to avoid metadata layering conflicts. If a column creation POST runs before its owning solution is imported, Dataverse may reject the operation or produce an orphaned component.

title: OData Type-Casting Constraints for FormulaDefinition
message: Cannot query FormulaDefinition on base AttributeMetadata. Append a derived type cast (e.g., /Microsoft.Dynamics.CRM.DecimalAttributeMetadata) before the query string.

title: Idempotency Gate Verification
message: Script lacks standard idempotency guards (e.g., if ($null -eq $existing)). Ensure Web API POSTs are guarded. Re-runs without a guard will create duplicate records in Dataverse.

title: Non-ASCII character in PowerShell double-quoted string literal
message: Non-ASCII character inside a double-quoted string literal will silently corrupt PowerShell 5.1 brace-counting and may produce MissingEndCurlyBrace at unrelated lines. Replace with ASCII (e.g., em-dash to '--', section sign to 'S', smart quotes to straight quotes).

title: Known-bad cmdlet+parameter combination
message: Cmdlet+parameter combination is known not to exist in the deployed module version. Verify with Get-Help or remove. (See: https://github.com/djwmobley/dataverse-linter for the full known-bad list.) Source: https://github.com/seanmcne/Microsoft.Xrm.Data.PowerShell/blob/master/Microsoft.Xrm.Data.PowerShell/Microsoft.Xrm.Data.PowerShell.psm1

title: Connect-PnPOnline -TenantId is not a valid parameter
message: Connect-PnPOnline does not have a -TenantId parameter. The correct parameter is -Tenant (accepts a tenant name such as mycompany.onmicrosoft.com). This will fail at runtime with a parameter-binding error. See: https://pnp.github.io/powershell/cmdlets/Connect-PnPOnline.html

title: Publish-PnPPage does not exist in PnP.PowerShell 3.x
message: Publish-PnPPage does not exist in PnP.PowerShell 3.x. Use Set-PnPPage -Identity <name> -Publish instead. This script will fail at runtime with a command-not-found error. See: https://pnp.github.io/powershell/cmdlets/Set-PnPPage.html

title: pac install is not a valid pac command group
message: pac install is not a recognized pac command group. The pac CLI has no 'install' top-level command. The closest valid surface is 'pac application install --environment-id <id> --application-name <name>'. This will fail at CLI parse time with 'unknown command'. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-platform/developer/cli/reference/

title: [Parser]::ParseFile with both output args as [ref]$null discards all diagnostics
message: [Parser]::ParseFile($p, [ref]$null, [ref]$null) silently discards all parsed tokens and all parse errors. Both 'tokens' and 'errors' are out parameters per the .NET API; passing [ref]$null writes them into unreachable references and the caller cannot inspect the parse result. Correct usage: $tokens=$null; $errs=$null; [Parser]::ParseFile($path, [ref]$tokens, [ref]$errs). See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.management.automation.language.parser.parsefile

title: [datetime]::TryParse with [ref]$null causes overload-resolution hazard
message: [datetime]::TryParse($s, [ref]$null) is an overload-resolution hazard. The 2-argument .NET overloads expect (String, out DateTime) or (ReadOnlySpan<Char>, out DateTime); passing [ref]$null makes the parsed DateTime value unreachable, and on .NET 7+ may resolve to a different overload and fail outright. Use '$result = $s -as [datetime]' instead: returns $null on failure with no exception and no overload ambiguity. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.datetime.tryparse

title: Manual [switch]$WhatIf parameter -- use [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess=$true)] + $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess()
description: A manual [switch]$WhatIf parameter bypasses PowerShell's CommonParameter injection: it does not set $WhatIfPreference for called cmdlets, does not wire -Confirm, and does not force destructive code through $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess() -- leaving side-effects free to fire regardless of WhatIf intent. The canonical fix is [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess=$true)], which removes the need for a manual switch and gates all writes through $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess($target, $operation).
remediation: Replace [switch]$WhatIf with [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess=$true)] on the enclosing script or function, and gate all writes through: if ($PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess($target, $operation)) { ... }
message: Manual [switch]$WhatIf detected without [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess=$true)]. A manual switch does not wire $WhatIfPreference, -Confirm, or $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess() -- side-effects will fire regardless of WhatIf intent. Add [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess=$true)] and gate all writes through $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess(). See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/scripting/learn/deep-dives/everything-about-shouldprocess

title: Variable followed by colon in double-quoted string -- scope-qualifier parse trap
description: Inside a double-quoted string, PowerShell treats $varname: as a scope-qualifier prefix (e.g., $env:, $script:, $global:). When the character after the colon is not a valid variable-name start (letter, digit, underscore, or opening brace for ${...}), the parser raises: 'Variable reference is not valid. ":" was not followed by a valid variable name character. Consider using ${} to delimit the name.' The script cannot load or run. Canonical fix: replace $varname: with ${varname}: -- the curly-brace form makes the colon literal and the variable name is unambiguous. Substrate citation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_quoting_rules -- 'This is especially important if the variable name is followed by a colon (:). PowerShell considers everything between the $ and the : a scope specifier, typically causing the interpretation to fail. For example, "$HOME: where the heart is." throws an error, but "${HOME}: where the heart is." works as intended.'
remediation: Replace $varname: with ${varname}: inside the double-quoted string to make the colon literal. Example: "FAILED on $ZipPath:`n" becomes "FAILED on ${ZipPath}:`n".
message: Variable followed by colon inside double-quoted string (e.g., "$varname: text"). PowerShell parses $varname: as a scope-qualifier prefix and raises a parse-time error when the char after ":" is not a valid variable-name start. The script cannot load. Fix: use ${varname}: to delimit the variable name and make ":" literal. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_quoting_rules

title: Invoke-PnPSPRestMethod -Url missing leading slash (P1)
message: Invoke-PnPSPRestMethod -Url value is missing a leading slash. PnP resolves -Url relative to the SharePoint site root only when it starts with '/'; a string that begins with a non-slash non-variable character is treated as a relative path and resolves incorrectly, producing a 404 or hitting the wrong resource. Fix: use -Url '/_api/...' (leading slash required). See: https://pnp.github.io/powershell/cmdlets/Invoke-PnPSPRestMethod.html

title: Dataverse _value suffix on SharePoint/ProjectServer REST URL (P2)
message: A Dataverse OData v4 '_<name>_value' lookup suffix appears in a SharePoint/Project Server REST URL. The '_value' suffix is specific to Dataverse Web API (OData v4); SharePoint and Project Server REST use OData v2/v3 where lookup columns are addressed by their plain field name (e.g., 'Owner', not '_owner_value'). Using '_value' on a /_api/ProjectServer/ or /_api/web/ endpoint produces a field-not-found error at runtime. Remove the '_value' suffix and use the plain OData v2/v3 field name. Sources: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sharepoint/dev/sp-add-ins/use-odata-query-operations-in-sharepoint-rest-requests and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-apps/developer/data-platform/webapi/query/overview

title: Connect-PnPOnline -DeviceLogin without -ClientId (P3)
message: Connect-PnPOnline -DeviceLogin is used without -ClientId. Since 2024-09-09 the shared PnP Management Shell multi-tenant Entra app was retired; -ClientId is now mandatory for all interactive and device-login authentication flows. Omitting -ClientId causes authentication to fail at runtime with an AADSTS error. Fix: register your own Entra app and supply -ClientId <your-app-id>. See: https://pnp.github.io/powershell/cmdlets/Connect-PnPOnline.html

title: Register-PnPManagementShellAccess is a removed cmdlet (P4)
message: Register-PnPManagementShellAccess has been removed from PnP.PowerShell. The shared multi-tenant PnP Management Shell Entra app it used to consent has been retired (2024-09-09). Using this cmdlet produces a CommandNotFoundException at runtime. The replacement is Register-PnPEntraIDAppForInteractiveLogin or Register-PnPEntraIDApp for registering your own app. See: https://pnp.github.io/powershell/articles/registerapplication.html

title: OData v4 type-cast syntax on SharePoint/ProjectServer REST endpoint (P8)
message: OData v4 type-cast syntax ('Namespace.Type/' segment in $select or $filter) appears in a SharePoint or Project Server REST URL. SharePoint and Project Server REST use OData v2/v3 which does not support type-cast segments; using 'Namespace.Type/' in a $select or $filter on a /_api/ProjectServer/ or /_api/web/ URL produces a parse error at runtime. Type casts in this form are a Dataverse OData v4 pattern and must not be used against SharePoint/ProjectServer endpoints. Sources: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sharepoint/dev/sp-add-ins/use-odata-query-operations-in-sharepoint-rest-requests and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-apps/developer/data-platform/webapi/query/overview

title: Statement keyword inside grouping operator -- use subexpression operator $(...) instead
description: The grouping operator ( ) treats its content as a command or expression, not a statement list. PowerShell statement keywords (if, foreach, for, while, switch, do, trap) placed immediately inside ( ) are parsed as command names -- the runtime raises 'The term "<kw>" is not recognized as a name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or executable program.' The parser does NOT catch this at parse time: [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseInput returns 0 errors, so AST-based parse checks are blind to this class of defect. The fix is the subexpression operator $( ) which is explicitly designed to hold one or more statements and return their output. Per about_Operators: grouping operator ( ) 'allows you to let output from a command participate in an expression'; subexpression operator $( ) 'returns the result of one or more statements'. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_operators
remediation: Replace the outer ( ) with $( ): change -Param (if (...) { ... } else { ... }) to -Param $(if (...) { ... } else { ... }). The array subexpression @( ) is also valid. Do not change normal statement usage 'if ($x) { ... }' -- that has the keyword BEFORE the paren, not after.
message: Statement keyword inside grouping operator '(...)' is parsed as a command and fails at runtime with 'The term is not recognized as a cmdlet'. The grouping operator ( ) holds expressions and commands only. Per about_Operators, $( ) is the subexpression operator that 'returns the result of one or more statements'. Use the subexpression operator $(...) instead. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_operators

title: [Math]::(Floor|Ceiling|Round) double passed to -f with integer format specifier :D/:X
message: [Math]::(Floor|Ceiling|Round) returns [double]; applying a :D or :X format specifier to a double throws 'Format specifier was invalid' at runtime. Cast before the -f operator: [int][Math]::Floor(...). .NET integer format specifiers (:D, :X) are valid only on integral types.
